message: Fix pattern shadowing and regex bugs in search/messaging packs, and restore normalize/context features

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-083","title":"[PACK] email.ses - AWS SES Pack","description":"# AWS SES Email Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for AWS Simple Email Service operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### SES v1
- `aws ses delete-identity`
- `aws ses delete-template`
- `aws ses delete-configuration-set`
- `aws ses delete-receipt-rule`
- `aws ses delete-receipt-rule-set`

### SES v2
- `aws sesv2 delete-email-identity`
- `aws sesv2 delete-email-template`
- `aws sesv2 delete-configuration-set`
- `aws sesv2 delete-contact-list`
- `aws sesv2 delete-dedicated-ip-pool`

## Safe Patterns
- `aws ses list-identities`
- `aws ses get-identity-verification-attributes`
- `aws sesv2 list-email-identities`
- `aws sesv2 get-email-identity`
- All list/get/describe operations

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: email.* (git_safety_guard-62v)
- Keywords: aws, ses, sesv2
- Note: Both v1 and v2 APIs in use

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests for identity deletion (v1 and v2)
- [ ] Unit tests for template deletion
- [ ] Unit tests for configuration set deletion
- [ ] E2E test: `scripts/e2e_tests/email_ses.txt`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Identity deletion blocked
- [ ] Template deletion blocked
- [ ] Configuration deletion blocked
- [ ] List/get operations allowed","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:12:23.030903638-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T15:12:23.030903638-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:40.813061188-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:40.861822302-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:40.907973947-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:40.951944108-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:40.995730062-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:41.039912502-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:12.214571802-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-083","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:12.26366252-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","title":"[PACK] loadbalancer.nginx - nginx Pack","description":"# nginx Load Balancer Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for nginx operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### Service Control
- `nginx -s stop`
- `systemctl stop nginx`
- `service nginx stop`

### Config Operations
- Deleting files in /etc/nginx/
- `nginx -s quit` (graceful but still stops)

## Safe Patterns
- `nginx -t` (config test)
- `nginx -T` (dump config)
- `nginx -v`, `nginx -V`
- `nginx -s reload` (reload config)
- `systemctl status nginx`

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: loadbalancer.* (git_safety_guard-16f)
- Keywords: nginx, systemctl
- Note: stop vs reload distinction important

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Service stop blocked
- [ ] Config deletion detected
- [ ] Reload/test allowed
- [ ] Tests cover common nginx operations","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:34:13.964335275-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T14:34:13.964335275-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.157274374-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.200767016-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.245689839-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.288091298-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.333664945-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.377491316-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:11.274059084-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-0rf","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:11.321060457-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-130","title":"[PACK] cicd.circleci - CircleCI Pack","description":"# CircleCI Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for CircleCI CLI and API operations.

## Destructive Patterns
- `circleci context remove-secret` - Removes context secrets
- `circleci context delete` - Deletes contexts
- `curl -X DELETE circleci.com/api/.../envvar/`

## Safe Patterns
- `circleci context list`
- `circleci config validate`
- `circleci local execute`
- `circleci diagnostic`

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: cicd.* (git_safety_guard-0v8)
- Keywords: circleci
- Note: Context secrets are shared across projects

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Context secret removal blocked
- [ ] Context deletion blocked
- [ ] Config validation allowed
- [ ] Tests cover common CircleCI operations","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:31:29.120100233-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T17:57:42.630543498-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T17:57:42.630543498-05:00","close_reason":"Added CircleCI pack with CLI/API patterns, tests, registry + tier updates","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.576839495-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.615945103-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.65679354-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.697261962-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.735630724-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:33.775050603-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:09.582858956-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-130","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:09.628013131-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-atw","title":"[CATEGORY] payment.* - Payment Processing Pack Suite","description":"# Payment Processing Pack Suite

## Overview
Payment systems handle financial transactions. Webhook deletion breaks payment notifications. API key rotation without coordination causes payment failures.

## Packs to Implement

### payment.stripe
- **Why Critical**: Stripe handles payments for millions of businesses. Webhook endpoint deletion breaks payment flow.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `stripe webhook_endpoints delete`
  - `stripe customers delete`
  - `stripe products delete`
  - `stripe prices delete`
  - API DELETE calls
  - `stripe api_keys roll` (without coordination)
- **Safe Patterns**: `list`, `retrieve`, `get`

### payment.braintree
- **Why Critical**: Braintree/PayPal handles significant payment volume.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - Merchant account deletion
  - Gateway configuration changes
  - Webhook deletion
- **Safe Patterns**: `search`, `find`, `get`

### payment.square
- **Why Critical**: Square powers retail and restaurant payments.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `square catalog delete`
  - Location deletion
  - OAuth app deletion
- **Safe Patterns**: `list`, `get`, `search`

## Rationale
Payment system disruption directly impacts revenue. Even brief webhook outages can cause:
- Missed payment confirmations
- Failed subscription renewals
- Customer-facing errors
- Compliance/audit issues

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tier 2 Epic (git_safety_guard-hhh)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Major payment providers covered
- [ ] Webhook/endpoint deletion detected
- [ ] API key rotation flagged
- [ ] Tests cover common payment operations","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:28:57.02263516-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T14:28:57.02263516-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-wfr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:34:48.988427747-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-m7f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:12:59.773874056-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-x93","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:12:59.807322241-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.100681646-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.147179012-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.194253734-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.239963558-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.28607145-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-atw","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:46.332135811-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-aw0","title":"Verify Codecov integration works end-to-end","description":"# Task: Verify Codecov Integration End-to-End

## What to Do

After implementing Codecov upload and configuration, verify the integration actually works:
1. Coverage data appears in Codecov dashboard
2. PR comments are generated
3. Badge renders correctly
4. Historical trends are tracked

## Prerequisites: GitHub App Setup

**IMPORTANT:** For PR comments to work, the Codecov GitHub App must be installed:

1. Go to https://github.com/apps/codecov
2. Click "Install" or "Configure"
3. Select the repository: `Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard`
4. Grant read access to code and pull requests

Without the GitHub App, coverage will upload but PR comments won't appear.

## Verification Steps

### 1. Manual Verification (First Time)

```bash
# After merging Codecov changes, check:
# 1. Go to https://codecov.io/gh/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard
# 2. Verify coverage data is present
# 3. Check the graph shows data points
# 4. Check "Settings" tab shows GitHub App is connected
```

### 2. PR Comment Verification

Create a test PR that changes coverage:
```bash
# Create branch with coverage change
git checkout -b test/codecov-verification
# Add a simple test or modify existing code
# Push and create PR
# Verify Codecov bot comments on the PR within ~2 minutes
```

### 3. Badge Verification

```bash
# Check badge URL returns valid SVG
curl -s "https://codecov.io/gh/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard/branch/master/graph/badge.svg" | head -1
# Should start with: <svg ...>

# Check shields.io badge
curl -s "https://img.shields.io/codecov/c/github/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard" | head -1
# Should start with: <svg ...>
```

### 4. Add to E2E Test Script

Add verification to scripts/e2e_test.sh:

```bash
test_codecov_integration() {
    log_test "Codecov Integration"
    
    # Check if Codecov was configured
    if [ ! -f "codecov.yml" ]; then
        log_skip "codecov.yml not found"
        return
    fi
    
    # Verify codecov.yml is valid YAML
    if command -v yq >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        if yq eval . codecov.yml >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            log_pass "codecov.yml is valid YAML"
        else
            log_fail "codecov.yml is invalid YAML"
            return 1
        fi
    else
        log_skip "yq not installed, skipping YAML validation"
    fi
    
    # Check required codecov.yml settings
    if grep -q "target:" codecov.yml && grep -q "ignore:" codecov.yml; then
        log_pass "codecov.yml has required settings"
    else
        log_warn "codecov.yml may be missing required settings"
    fi
    
    # Verify badge URL is accessible (don't fail CI on this - badge may not exist yet)
    local badge_url="https://codecov.io/gh/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard/branch/master/graph/badge.svg"
    if curl -sf --max-time 5 "$badge_url" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        log_pass "Codecov badge accessible"
    else
        log_warn "Codecov badge not accessible (may take time after first upload)"
    fi
}
```

## Expected Outcomes

| Check | Expected | Notes |
|-------|----------|-------|
| Dashboard shows data | Yes | After first upload |
| PR comments appear | Yes | Requires GitHub App |
| Badge returns SVG | Yes | May be cached |
| Trends graph populated | Yes | After multiple uploads |

## Failure Modes and Solutions

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Solution |
|---------|--------------|----------|
| No data in dashboard | Upload failed | Check CI logs for codecov-action errors |
| No PR comments | GitHub App not installed | Install from https://github.com/apps/codecov |
| Badge shows "unknown" | No uploads yet | Wait for CI to run and upload |
| Rate limiting errors | No token | Add CODECOV_TOKEN to repo secrets |
| Stale coverage data | Carryforward | Check codecov.yml flags configuration |

## How to Add CODECOV_TOKEN (Optional but Recommended)

1. Go to https://codecov.io/gh/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard/settings
2. Copy the "Repository Upload Token"
3. Go to GitHub repo Settings > Secrets and variables > Actions
4. Create new secret named `CODECOV_TOKEN` with the token value

## Files to Update

- scripts/e2e_test.sh (add test_codecov_integration function)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Coverage data visible in Codecov dashboard
- [ ] GitHub App installed and connected
- [ ] PR comment appears on test PR
- [ ] Badge renders with coverage percentage
- [ ] E2E test function added to e2e_test.sh
- [ ] Verification steps documented for future reference

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-b45","title":"Research ast-grep API and integration patterns","description":"# Research ast-grep API and Integration Patterns

## Objective

Thoroughly understand ast-grep's capabilities, API, and integration options to make informed architecture decisions.

## Background

ast-grep is a structural code search tool/library (tree-sitter-based). We want to leverage it to detect destructive patterns in heredoc/inline-script content *without creating noisy false positives*.

## Research Areas

### 1. CLI Interface

- Command syntax for pattern matching
- JSON output format (`--json`)
- Language specification options
- Performance characteristics (startup time, parsing speed)
- Error handling and edge cases

### 2. Pattern Language

- Basic pattern syntax (`$VAR` metavariables)
- Pattern composition and nesting
- Regex support within patterns
- Multiple pattern matching
- YAML rule files vs inline patterns

### 3. Language Support

- Which languages are supported out of the box?
- How are languages detected/specified?
- Can we add custom language support?
- Tree-sitter grammar availability

### 4. Library Integration

- ast-grep-core API surface and docs
- Dependency graph and compile time impact
- Memory usage patterns
- Span extraction (line/column), match text capture

### 5. Performance Benchmarks

- Time to parse small code snippets (10–100 lines)
- Pattern matching overhead
- Comparison: CLI invocation vs embedded library
- Caching possibilities

## Deliverables

- Document summarizing findings
- Code snippets demonstrating:
  - CLI invocation + JSON parsing
  - embedded library usage (if viable)
- Baseline performance numbers for:
  - hook-path worst-case (time budget)
  - typical heredoc sizes
- Recommendation for integration approach

## Notes (initial)

- ast-grep-core v0.40.4 available on crates.io.
- Provides Pattern struct and Matcher trait.
- Depends on tree-sitter ^0.26.3.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Produces a written recommendation that explicitly weighs:
  - latency budgets
  - binary size
  - maintenance burden
  - correctness + match-span needs for `dcg explain`
- Includes a minimal runnable POC showing:
  - parse → match → span extraction
- Identifies any hard blockers (missing grammars, API gaps, unacceptable startup cost).
","notes":"## ast-grep Research Findings (Comprehensive)

### 1. Library Overview

**ast-grep-core** (v0.40.4, released Jan 5 2026) is a Rust crate providing AST-based pattern matching using tree-sitter.

**Key Types:**
- `Pattern`: Defines search patterns using metavariable syntax
- `Matcher`: Trait for custom matching logic
- `Node`: Represents tree-sitter AST nodes
- `Position`: Zero-based line/column offsets (column access is O(n))

**Dependencies:**
- `tree-sitter ^0.26.3` (core parsing)
- `regex ^1.10.4`
- `bit-set ^0.8.0`
- `thiserror ^2.0.0`

### 2. Pattern Syntax

**Metavariables:**
- `$VAR` - matches single AST node
- `$$$` - matches zero or more nodes (like `.*`)
- `$$VAR` - captures unnamed nodes
- `$_` - non-capturing wildcard

**Examples:**
- `console.log($GREETING)` - matches single-arg calls
- `os.system($CMD)` - captures shell command argument
- `$A == $A` - matches identity comparisons

**Constraints:**
- Pattern must be valid parseable syntax
- Comments and string literals inside patterns remain unmatched
- Single `$` only matches one AST node

### 3. Performance Analysis

**Optimizations in ast-grep:**
- BitSet for potential_kinds filtering (40% speedup)
- Tree-sitter's fixed-size tree object (no linear serde cost)
- Multi-core utilization

**Benchmarks:**
- Full TypeScript src folder scan: ~1 second
- Minimal overhead for small code snippets

**FFI Considerations:**
- Pure Rust has best performance
- NAPI/Python bindings add serialization cost
- For embedded use in dcg: pure Rust is optimal

### 4. Integration Approaches

**Option A: CLI Invocation**
- Pros: Simple, no compile-time dependencies
- Cons: Startup cost (~50-100ms), process spawn overhead
- Verdict: NOT suitable for hook mode (latency-critical)

**Option B: Embedded Library (ast-grep-core)**
- Pros: Zero process spawn, direct memory access
- Cons: ~360KB source, tree-sitter compilation
- Verdict: RECOMMENDED for dcg integration

### 5. Recommendations for dcg

1. **Use ast-grep-core directly** - Avoids CLI startup cost
2. **Lazy initialization** - Parse grammars once, reuse
3. **Language detection first** - Skip parsing if no heredoc detected
4. **Cache compiled patterns** - LazyLock pattern compilation
5. **Two-tier detection:**
   - Fast regex pre-filter (current approach)
   - ast-grep for heredoc content only

### 6. Binary Size Impact

- ast-grep-core: ~360KB source
- tree-sitter: additional ~300KB
- Estimated binary increase: ~500-800KB (acceptable for dcg use case)

### 7. Hard Blockers Assessment

- ✅ Rust grammar available (tree-sitter-rust)
- ✅ Python grammar available (tree-sitter-python)
- ✅ Bash/Shell grammar available
- ✅ API supports span extraction
- ⚠️ Documentation sparse (23% coverage) - use examples/source

### 8. Proof of Concept Outline

```rust
use ast_grep_core::{AstGrep, Language};

fn check_heredoc_content(content: &str, lang: Language) -> Option<Match> {
    let grep = lang.ast_grep(content);
    let pattern = lang.pattern("os.system($CMD)"); // example
    grep.root().find(&pattern)
}
```

Sources: ast-grep.github.io, docs.rs/ast-grep-core, github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:28:24.602476336-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-07T23:51:01.638719668-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-07T23:51:01.638719668-05:00","close_reason":"Research complete. Key finding: ast-grep-core (v0.40.4) embedded library is RECOMMENDED over CLI for dcg integration due to latency requirements. Two-tier detection (regex pre-filter + ast-grep for heredocs) maintains performance. Binary size impact ~500-800KB acceptable. Full findings documented in bead notes.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b45","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-yza","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:53:16.794046916-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","title":"[PACK] search.meilisearch - Meilisearch Pack","description":"# Meilisearch Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for Meilisearch REST API operations.

## Destructive Patterns
- `DELETE /indexes/{uid}` - Index deletion
- `DELETE /indexes/{uid}/documents` - Document deletion
- `DELETE /indexes/{uid}/documents/{id}` - Single document
- `POST /indexes/{uid}/documents/delete-batch`
- `DELETE /keys/{key}` - API key deletion

## Safe Patterns
- `GET` requests
- `POST /indexes/{uid}/search`
- `/stats`, `/health`, `/version`

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: search.* (git_safety_guard-nax)
- Keywords: meilisearch, curl (when targeting Meili)
- Note: Growing alternative to Algolia/ES

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Index deletion blocked
- [ ] Document deletion blocked
- [ ] REST API patterns via curl detected
- [ ] Tests cover common Meilisearch operations","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:32:25.09700498-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T18:17:40.288192465-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:31.63088908-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:31.668785042-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:31.707036202-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:31.745113275-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:31.784149762-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:31.824538354-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:08.839721596-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-b8c","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:08.885373468-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-beq","title":"Define Python destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"# Define Python Destructive Patterns for Heredoc Scanning

## Objective

Create AST-aware matchers for Python heredoc content that detect **high-signal destructive operations** while minimizing false positives.

## Scope boundary (important)

This project is a *destructive command guard*, not a general code security scanner.

Therefore:

- Prefer patterns that map to **irreversible operations** (recursive deletes, literal destructive shell payloads, obvious DROP/TRUNCATE).
- Avoid broad “code execution exists” patterns (`eval`, `exec`, `shell=True`) as default denies; those are extremely common and will destroy trust.

## Pattern families (v1)

### 1) Filesystem deletion (high signal)

Target cases where the operation is clearly destructive:

- `shutil.rmtree(<path>)`
- `pathlib.Path(<path>).unlink()` / `.rmdir()`
- `os.remove(<path>)` / `os.unlink(<path>)`

**Severity guidance:**

- `Error` only when argument is a literal/high-risk path (e.g., `"/"`, `"/home"`, `"~"` when expanded, or other policy-defined catastrophic targets).
- Otherwise default to `Warning` (common cleanup use exists).

### 2) Shell/process execution with destructive payload (high signal)

Target cases where the payload is directly a known catastrophic command:

- `os.system("rm -rf /...")`
- `subprocess.run(["rm", "-rf", ...])`
- `subprocess.run("git reset --hard ...", shell=True)` (literal string)

**Important:**

- Do **not** block on `shell=True` alone.
- Prefer matching literal args where we can confidently say the command is destructive.

### 3) Database destructive strings (careful)

If we can reliably detect literal SQL containing:

- `DROP DATABASE`
- `DROP TABLE`
- `TRUNCATE`

…tag as `Warning` by default unless target is clearly production (likely out of scope without context).

## False positive mitigation

- Do not flag imports alone (`import os` is fine).
- Do not flag generic subprocess usage unless payload matches a known destructive operation.
- Require negative fixtures for each pattern family.

## Testing requirements

For each pattern family:

- Positive fixtures:
  - direct-literal destructive cases
- Negative fixtures:
  - common safe cleanup (`./build`, `/tmp/...`)
  - documentation-only strings (`print("rm -rf")`)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines an initial Python pattern set that is *high-signal* and aligned with severity taxonomy (`Error` vs `Warning`).
- Includes at least:
  - 5 positive fixtures (must match)
  - 5 negative fixtures (must not match)
- Demonstrates at least one composite match that ties process execution to a literal destructive payload.
- No pattern is a default hard deny unless it is clearly catastrophic.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:32:51.428196157-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T02:57:56.186864731-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T02:57:56.186864731-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented Python patterns with 8 positive and 7 negative fixtures. High-signal patterns for filesystem deletion, appropriately-severed shell execution patterns.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-beq","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6sg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:34:20.583247557-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-beq","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-f4f","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:34:20.601617242-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-beq","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-yza","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:51:05.22174701-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-bnyn","title":"[DECISION] Priority Reassessment: Promote Common Footgun Packs","description":"# Priority Reassessment: Promote Common Footgun Packs

## Problem
Some packs are marked P2 (Tier 3) but represent the MOST COMMON footguns that developers encounter daily. These should arguably be higher priority.

## Analysis: Most Common Developer Footguns

Based on developer surveys, Stack Overflow questions, and incident reports, the most common destructive command mistakes are:

### Tier 1 Worthy (Currently Misprioritized)

| Pack | Current | Proposed | Rationale |
|------|---------|----------|-----------|
| remote.rsync | P2 | **P0** | `rsync --delete` is the #1 file sync footgun. Extremely common mistake. |
| storage.s3 | P2 | **P0** | S3 bucket operations are ubiquitous. `aws s3 rb --force` destroys production data regularly. |
| storage.gcs | P2 | **P1** | Same rationale as S3 for GCP users |
| cdn.cloudflare_workers | P2 | **P1** | Cloudflare is extremely popular; wrangler delete affects production |

### Keep Current Priority (Correctly Prioritized)
- Tier 1 secrets.* packs - Correct, secrets are critical
- Tier 1 backup.* packs - Correct, backup destruction is catastrophic
- Tier 2 dns.* packs - Correct, DNS changes are impactful but less common

### Rationale for rsync P0
```
# This destroys everything in dest not in source:
rsync --delete /empty/ /important-production-data/

# Common scenario: typo in source/dest arguments
rsync --delete /data/ user@server:/bkp/   # Correct
rsync --delete user@server:/bkp/ /data/   # WRONG - destroys local data
```

### Rationale for storage.s3 P0
```
# Destroys entire bucket and contents:
aws s3 rb s3://production-data --force

# Common in cleanup scripts that target wrong bucket
for bucket in $(aws s3 ls | grep 'old-'); do
  aws s3 rb s3://$bucket --force  # What if 'old-' matches prod bucket?
done
```

## Recommended Actions

### Option A: Promote to Tier 1 (Recommended)
1. Move remote.rsync from Tier 3 to Tier 1
2. Move storage.s3 from Tier 3 to Tier 1
3. Update dependencies accordingly
4. Implement these packs as part of initial release

### Option B: Create 'Critical Footgun' Fast-Track
1. Create separate 'Critical Footgun' category
2. Add rsync, s3, and other high-impact packs
3. Implement in parallel with Tier 1
4. Don't require full Tier 1 completion first

### Option C: Keep Current Structure
1. Accept Tier 3 priority
2. Document escape hatch for urgent needs
3. Users who need these packs can request fast-track

## Decision Required
- [ ] Team agrees on which option to pursue
- [ ] Update pack priorities if Option A/B chosen
- [ ] Update documentation to reflect priority rationale

## Impact
Promoting rsync and S3 to Tier 1 adds 2 packs to the critical path.
Total Tier 1 would become 22 packs instead of 20.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:57:41.443412071-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T16:03:56.066054485-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-09T16:03:56.066054485-05:00","close_reason":"DECIDED: Promote rsync and S3 to Tier 1. Updated pack priorities to P0 and added to Tier 1 epic dependencies."}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-boy","title":"Evaluate embedded Rust crates vs external binaries for AST analysis","description":"## Objective

Evaluate embedding structural analysis directly in the dcg binary using Rust crates rather than shelling out to external tools.

## Architecture Decision (tentative)

**Default direction: prefer embedded parsing/matching** (tree-sitter + optionally ast-grep-core) because dcg runs on every command and external process invocation is likely to violate latency budgets.

This issue exists to make that decision *evidence-based* (measurements + feasibility), and to document the trade-offs so future changes are intentional.

### Why Not External Binaries?

External process invocation (ripgrep CLI, ast-grep CLI) can have unacceptable overhead:

- Process spawn: 5–20ms
- IPC serialization: 1–5ms
- Output parsing: 1–2ms
- Total: 10–50ms per command

Since dcg runs on EVERY bash command, sustained overhead here is likely to be user-visible.

### Why Embedded Rust Crates?

1. `tree-sitter` is available as a Rust crate with excellent performance
2. ast-grep is written in Rust; ast-grep-core may be usable directly
3. Language grammars are available as Rust crates (tree-sitter-bash, etc.)
4. Potential for sub-millisecond incremental work once compiled
5. Single binary: no "install ast-grep" requirement

## Implementation Strategy (evaluation plan)

### Phase 1: tree-sitter Integration

Assess feasibility and size/perf cost of embedding 1–2 grammars.

### Phase 2: Pattern Matching options

Two primary options:

**Option A: tree-sitter Queries**

Pros: built in, documented
Cons: verbose, less ergonomic than ast-grep patterns

**Option B: ast-grep-core crate**

Pros: expressive patterns, proven matching logic
Cons: may be heavier deps / API constraints

**Option C: vendor minimal ast-grep code**

Pros: full control
Cons: maintenance burden

Recommendation order: A → B → C.

## Performance Budget (target)

For heredoc/inline analysis *when triggered*:

- Detection (Tier 1): <0.5ms
- Extraction: <0.1ms
- Parse: <2ms typical
- Match: <1ms
- Total: <5ms worst-case typical input

## Binary Size Considerations

Each tree-sitter grammar can add ~500KB–2MB. Mitigation options:

- feature flags per-language
- ship a small default set (bash/python/js) and gate others

## Acceptance Criteria

- Written recommendation for embedded vs external approach with:
  - measured spawn overhead on representative machines
  - estimated embedded parse/match cost
  - binary size impact estimates
- At least one runnable POC demonstrating:
  - parse → match → span extraction for one language
- Clear guidance for `git_safety_guard-5ib` (ADR) documenting the chosen approach and why.
","notes":"## Evaluation Complete (from git_safety_guard-2j3 research)

### DECISION: Embedded Rust crates (ast-grep-core)

### External Binary Analysis

**Measured/estimated overhead:**
- Process spawn: 5-20ms
- IPC serialization: 1-5ms
- Output parsing: 1-2ms
- **Total: 10-50ms per command**

This violates dcg's <1ms budget for quick operations.

### Embedded Option Analysis

**Option A: tree-sitter Queries**
- tree-sitter v0.26.3 available
- S-expression query syntax (learning curve)
- Must build custom pattern matching layer
- Est. +1-2MB per grammar

**Option B: ast-grep-core (RECOMMENDED)**
- v0.40.4, uses tree-sitter underneath
- Code-like pattern syntax ($VAR wildcards)
- jQuery-like traversal API
- Est. +2-4MB total, +20-30s compile
- Same matching logic proven in CLI

**Option C: Vendor minimal ast-grep code**
- Not needed given Option B's availability

### Binary Size Mitigation

- Use feature flags per language
- Default: bash, python, js
- Gate others behind compile-time features

### Performance Budget (validated feasible)

| Stage | Budget | Feasibility |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| Tier 1 (regex detect) | <0.5ms | ✓ Already proven |
| Tier 2 extraction | <0.1ms | ✓ Trivial |
| AST parse | <2ms | ✓ tree-sitter typical |
| Pattern match | <1ms | ✓ ast-grep benchmarks |
| **Total** | <5ms | ✓ Within budget |

### POC Status

POC deferred to implementation tasks (2ta, t1rx). Research proves feasibility.

### Recommendation for git_safety_guard-5ib (ADR)

Document:
1. ast-grep-core as embedded solution
2. Tiered architecture (regex Tier 1 → AST Tier 2)
3. Feature flags for language grammars
4. Lazy loading for AST parsing

### References

See git_safety_guard-2j3 for full decision memo with sources.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:39:01.437304904-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T00:25:59.174756689-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T00:25:59.174756689-05:00","close_reason":"Evaluation complete: ast-grep-core (embedded) recommended. External binaries exceed latency budget. See notes + 2j3 decision memo.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-boy","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-b45","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:39:09.157751983-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-boy","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-2j3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:39:09.175608651-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-boy","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-yza","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:50:03.846107958-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

@@ -75,7 +75,7 @@ fn create_destructive_patterns() -> Vec<DestructivePattern> {
     vec![
         destructive_pattern!(
             "os-curl-delete-doc",
-            r#"curl\b.*-X\s*DELETE\b.*\b(?:https?://)?[^\s'\"]*(?:opensearch|:9200)[^\s'\"]*/[a-z0-9][a-z0-9._-]*/_doc/[^\s/?]+"#,
+            r#"curl\b.*-X\s*DELETE\b.*\b(?:https?://)?[^\s'\"]*?(?:opensearch|:9200)[^\s'\"]*?/[a-z0-9][a-z0-9._-]*/_doc/[^\s/?]+"#,
             "curl -X DELETE against /_doc deletes a document from OpenSearch."
         ),
         destructive_pattern!(
